data.content:
### Create IAM Role for EC2 Bedrock Access (AWS CLI)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/bedrock.md

These AWS CLI commands demonstrate the process of creating an IAM role (`EC2-Bedrock-Access`) with an assume role policy allowing EC2 instances to assume it. It then attaches the `AmazonBedrockFullAccess` managed policy to grant necessary Bedrock permissions and creates an instance profile. Finally, it associates the instance profile with a specified EC2 instance.

```bash
# 1. Create IAM role and instance profile
aws iam create-role --role-name EC2-Bedrock-Access \
  --assume-role-policy-document '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {"Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"},
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  }'

aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name EC2-Bedrock-Access \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonBedrockFullAccess

aws iam create-instance-profile --instance-profile-name EC2-Bedrock-Access
aws iam add-role-to-instance-profile \
  --instance-profile-name EC2-Bedrock-Access \
  --role-name EC2-Bedrock-Access

# 2. Attach to your EC2 instance
aws ec2 associate-iam-instance-profile \
  --instance-id i-xxxxx \
  --iam-instance-profile Name=EC2-Bedrock-Access

# 3. On the EC2 instance, enable discovery
openclaw config set models.bedrockDiscovery.enabled true
openclaw config set models.bedrockDiscovery.region us-east-1

# 4. Set the workaround env vars
echo 'export AWS_PROFILE=default' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export AWS_REGION=us-east-1' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# 5. Verify models are discovered
openclaw models list
```

--------------------------------

### EC2 Instance Role Workaround for OpenClaw (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/bedrock.md

This bash snippet provides a workaround for OpenClaw's credential detection when using EC2 instance roles for AWS authentication. By setting `AWS_PROFILE=default` and `AWS_REGION`, you signal to the AWS SDK that credentials are available, even though OpenClaw's direct check might miss IMDS credentials. The actual authentication still relies on the attached IAM role.

```bash
# Add to ~/.bashrc or your shell profile
export AWS_PROFILE=default
export AWS_REGION=us-east-1
```

--------------------------------

### Deploy OpenClaw Application (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This command initiates the deployment of the OpenClaw application to Fly.io. The first deployment will build the Docker image, which may take a few minutes. Subsequent deployments are typically faster.

```bash
fly deploy
```

--------------------------------

### Convert to Private Deployment (Release IPs)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

These commands demonstrate how to transition an existing Openclaw deployment to a private configuration by releasing public IP addresses and then deploying with the private template.

```bash
# List current IPs
fly ips list -a my-openclaw

# Release public IPs
fly ips release <public-ipv4> -a my-openclaw
fly ips release <public-ipv6> -a my-openclaw

# Deploy with private config
fly deploy -c fly.private.toml
```

--------------------------------

### Deploy Openclaw with Private Configuration

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This command deploys Openclaw using a private configuration file (`fly.private.toml`), which is essential for hardened deployments that avoid public IP allocation.

```bash
fly deploy -c fly.private.toml
```

--------------------------------

### Set AWS Credentials for Bedrock (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/bedrock.md

These bash commands set environment variables for AWS authentication, which are used by the AWS SDK for accessing Amazon Bedrock. This includes access keys, secret access keys, region, and optionally session tokens or a Bedrock bearer token. These are typically set on the gateway host where OpenClaw is running.

```bash
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="AKIA..."
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="..."
export AWS_REGION="us-east-1"
# Optional:
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN="..."
export AWS_PROFILE="your-profile"
# Optional (Bedrock API key/bearer token):
export AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK="..."
```

--------------------------------

### Access Private Deployment via Local Proxy

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This is the simplest method to access a private Openclaw deployment. It forwards a local port to the application, allowing you to access it via `localhost` in your browser.

```bash
# Forward local port 3000 to the app
fly proxy 3000:3000 -a my-openclaw

# Then open http://localhost:3000 in browser
```

--------------------------------

### Update Openclaw Deployment

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

These commands guide you through updating your Openclaw deployment. It involves pulling the latest code, redeploying the application, and checking its status and logs.

```bash
git pull
fly deploy
fly status
fly logs
```

--------------------------------

### Allocate Private IPv6

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

After converting to a private deployment, this command allocates a private-only IPv6 address to your application, ensuring it remains inaccessible from the public internet.

```bash
fly ips allocate-v6 --private -a my-openclaw
```

--------------------------------

### Write Openclaw Config via SFTP

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This demonstrates using `fly sftp` to transfer a local configuration file to the remote Openclaw deployment. It also includes a command to remove the existing config file if necessary before uploading.

```bash
# On the remote machine:
fly sftp shell
> put /local/path/config.json /data/openclaw.json

# To remove existing file first:
fly ssh console --command "rm /data/openclaw.json"
```

--------------------------------

### Create WireGuard Config for Private Access

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

For accessing a private deployment, you can create a WireGuard configuration. This config can then be imported into your WireGuard client, allowing access via the internal IPv6 address.

```bash
fly wireguard create
```

--------------------------------

### Update Openclaw Machine Startup Command

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This allows you to change the command that starts your Openclaw application on a specific machine without performing a full `fly deploy`. You can also combine this with memory updates.

```bash
# Get machine ID
fly machines list

# Update command
fly machine update <machine-id> --command "node dist/index.js gateway --port 3000 --bind lan" -y

# Update command and memory
fly machine update <machine-id> --vm-memory 2048 --command "node dist/index.js gateway --port 3000 --bind lan" -y
```

--------------------------------

### Access Openclaw SSH Console

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This command opens an SSH console to your Openclaw deployment, enabling direct interaction with the container for debugging or management tasks.

```bash
fly ssh console
```

--------------------------------

### Set Openclaw State Directory

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

To ensure state (like credentials or sessions) persists across restarts, set the `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` environment variable to `/data` in your `fly.toml` file and redeploy.

```toml
# Example fly.toml snippet
[env]
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR = "/data"
```

--------------------------------

### Create Fly App and Volume (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This snippet demonstrates creating a new Fly.io application and a persistent volume for storing OpenClaw data. It requires the flyctl CLI and assumes you have cloned the OpenClaw repository.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git
cd openclaw

# Create a new Fly app (pick your own name)
fly apps create my-openclaw

# Create a persistent volume (1GB is usually enough)
fly volumes create openclaw_data --size 1 --region iad
```

--------------------------------

### Render Blueprint Configuration (YAML)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/render.mdx

Defines the OpenClaw service on Render, including runtime, environment variables, and persistent disk storage. This YAML file configures the web service type, Docker runtime, health check path, environment variables such as PORT and SETUP_PASSWORD, and a persistent disk mounted at /data.

```yaml
services:
  - type: web
    name: openclaw
    runtime: docker
    plan: starter
    healthCheckPath: /health
    envVars:
      - key: PORT
        value: "8080"
      - key: SETUP_PASSWORD
        sync: false          # prompts during deploy
      - key: OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR
        value: /data/.openclaw
      - key: OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE_DIR
        value: /data/workspace
      - key: OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN
        generateValue: true  # auto-generates a secure token
    disk:
      name: openclaw-data
      mountPath: /data
      sizeGB: 1
```

--------------------------------

### Update Machine Memory via CLI

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This command allows you to update the memory allocated to a specific Fly machine without requiring a full redeploy. Replace `<machine-id>` with your actual machine identifier.

```bash
fly machine update <machine-id> --vm-memory 2048 -y
```

--------------------------------

### Create OpenClaw Configuration File (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This snippet shows how to SSH into a Fly.io machine and create the `openclaw.json` configuration file within the persistent volume. It defines agents, authentication profiles, bindings, channel configurations (Discord), and gateway settings.

```bash
fly ssh console

mkdir -p /data
cat > /data/openclaw.json << 'EOF'
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "model": {
        "primary": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-5",
        "fallbacks": ["anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5", "openai/gpt-4o"]
      },
      "maxConcurrent": 4
    },
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "default": true
      }
    ]
  },
  "auth": {
    "profiles": {
      "anthropic:default": { "mode": "token", "provider": "anthropic" },
      "openai:default": { "mode": "token", "provider": "openai" }
    }
  },
  "bindings": [
    {
      "agentId": "main",
      "match": { "channel": "discord" }
    }
  ],
  "channels": {
    "discord": {
      "enabled": true,
      "groupPolicy": "allowlist",
      "guilds": {
        "YOUR_GUILD_ID": {
          "channels": { "general": { "allow": true } },
          "requireMention": false
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "gateway": {
    "mode": "local",
    "bind": "auto"
  },
  "meta": {
    "lastTouchedVersion": "2026.1.29"
  }
}
EOF
```

--------------------------------

### Write Openclaw Config via SSH (echo + tee)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This method uses `echo` to provide the configuration content and `tee` to write it to the specified file on the remote machine. It's a way to write files when shell redirection is not directly supported.

```bash
echo '{"your":"config"}' | fly ssh console -C "tee /data/openclaw.json"
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Bedrock Automatic Model Discovery (JSON5)

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/bedrock.md

This JSON5 configuration enables automatic discovery of streaming and text-output capable Bedrock models. It allows filtering by provider, setting a refresh interval for cached model lists, and defining default context window and max tokens for discovered models. The `enabled` field defaults to true if AWS credentials are found.

```json5
{
  models: {
    bedrockDiscovery: {
      enabled: true,
      region: "us-east-1",
      providerFilter: ["anthropic", "amazon"],
      refreshInterval: 3600,
      defaultContextWindow: 32000,
      defaultMaxTokens: 4096
    }
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Verify Openclaw Config File

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

This command checks if your custom Openclaw configuration file (`/data/openclaw.json`) exists within the container. It's useful for troubleshooting issues where the gateway might be using a default configuration.

```bash
fly ssh console --command "cat /data/openclaw.json"
```

--------------------------------

### Remove Gateway Lock File via SSH

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/platforms/fly.md

If the gateway fails to start due to a persistent lock file, this command connects via SSH and removes the lock file. A subsequent machine restart is required.

```bash
fly ssh console --command "rm -f /data/gateway.*.lock"
fly machine restart <machine-id>
```

--------------------------------

### Install and Configure Tailscale on VPS

Source: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/help/faq.md

Installs Tailscale on a Virtual Private Server (VPS) using a script and enables it for use. This allows the VPS to join a Tailscale network for secure access.

```bash
curl -fsSL https://tailscale.com/install.sh | sh
sudo tailscale up
```
